cheque_bounce_138.display_name: Cheque Bounce Demand Notice (Section 138, Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881)

claims.claim: Notice must be sent within 30 days of receipt of the cheque return memo (S.138 proviso (b))
Drawer has 15 days from receipt of notice to pay (S.138 proviso (c))
Cheque validity is 3 months from its date per RBI directive
claims.note: Reduced from 6 months w.e.f. 01.04.2012; encoded only as date-offset ranges, not cited in documents

claims.claim: A leave and license is a 'license' as defined in Section 52, Indian Easements Act, 1882, creating no interest in immovable property; juridical possession remains with the licensor
claims.note: Cited in statutory_requirements and recitals guidance; no regex enforces the section number

claims.claim: Section 55, Maharashtra Rent Control Act, 1999 requires leave and license agreements to be in writing and registered, with responsibility on the landlord/licensor
claims.note: Only the Act name+year (not the section number) is enforced via the mrc_act_citation regex

claims.claim: Non-registration under Section 55 is punishable (commonly stated as imprisonment up to 3 months or fine up to Rs 5,000)
claims.note: Quantum flagged [VERIFY] in statutory_requirements; never required in generated documents

claims.claim: Stamp duty on leave and license is governed by the Maharashtra Stamp Act, 1958
claims.note: Act name only; no article or rate enforced anywhere

claims.claim: The applicable stamp article is 36A with a 0.25% computation on rent plus deposit components
claims.note: Tagged [VERIFY] in statutory_requirements; excluded from all regexes and document text

claims.claim: Maharashtra permits online e-registration of leave and license agreements via the Department of Registration and Stamps (IGR Maharashtra)
claims.note: Administrative practice claim; no statute cited

claims.claim: Intimation/verification of licensee particulars with the local police is required or customary in Maharashtra cities
claims.note: Usually traced to Police Commissioner orders (CrPC s.144) / Maharashtra Police Act; included only as an optional customary clause with [VERIFY] tag

claims.claim: Registration Act, 1908 (S.17) interplay with licenses and the registration machinery before the Sub-Registrar
claims.note: Mentioned only with a [VERIFY] tag; no section number in any regex

claims.claim: MRCA 1999 exemptions (S.3, e.g., companies above a paid-up capital threshold) may affect applicability of the Act to corporate licensees
claims.note: The MRCA citation gate is retained for company scenarios as universal Maharashtra registration practice; flag for legal review

claims.claim: Licensor/Licensee terminology and 'license fee' (never lessor/lessee or monthly rent) preserve the license character of the document
claims.note: Drafting convention, not a statutory citation; enforced via required parties_terminology/license_fee_clause patterns and the no_lessor_lessee/no_monthly_rent forbidden patterns

legal_notice_money_recovery.display_name: Legal Notice for Recovery of Money / Outstanding Dues

claims.claim: Most money claims are time-barred three years from when the debt falls due, under the Limitation Act, 1963
claims.note: Cited only generally in statutory_requirements; specific Schedule Article numbers are deliberately not cited and are blocked by the no_limitation_article_numbers forbidden pattern

claims.claim: A summary suit under Order XXXVII, Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 is available for liquidated money demands arising on written contracts
claims.note: Optional reference only — appears in statutory_requirements and as one alternation in the civil_suit_consequence required pattern, never as a mandatory citation

claims.claim: The 15- or 30-day compliance window in a money-recovery notice is customary drafting practice, not a statutory requirement
claims.note: The compliance_window_days required pattern enforces only that a window is stated, not any statutory basis

claims.claim: Where no contractual rate exists, interest may be claimed from the date of written demand under the Interest Act, 1978
claims.note: The Act exists, but its precise conditions and rate mechanics are unverified; flagged [VERIFY] in statutory_requirements and not encoded in any regex

claims.claim: Registered MSME suppliers may invoke statutory interest on delayed payments under the MSMED Act, 2006 (commonly cited as Ss. 15-16, compound interest at 3x bank rate)
claims.note: Section numbers and rate flagged [VERIFY] in statutory_requirements; never baked into a required pattern

claims.claim: The Indian Penal Code stands repealed and replaced by the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 with effect from 1 July 2024, so IPC/Section 420 citations are forbidden
claims.note: Basis for the no_repealed_ipc forbidden pattern; also discourages coercive criminal threats in a civil demand

claims.claim: Section 138, Negotiable Instruments Act 1881 is confined to cheque dishonour and does not belong in a generic money-recovery notice
claims.note: Basis for the no_sec_138_drift forbidden pattern; prevents cross-type hallucination since no cheque facts are generated

claims.claim: Date anchors (debt origin -1000 to -240 days, due date +30 to +180 days after origin) keep the claim within the 3-year limitation and the default already crystallised at notice date
claims.note: Encoded only as anchor/offset ranges, never cited in the documents themselves

legal_notice_landlord_tenant.display_name: Landlord-Tenant Legal Notice (Eviction / Rent Arrears / Deposit Refund / Essential Services)

claims.claim: A lease from month to month is terminable by 15 days' notice under Section 106, Transfer of Property Act, 1882
claims.note: Post-2002-amendment position. Stated in statutory_requirements only; not enforced by any regex because it applies only to landlord termination notices, not tenant-direction notices.

claims.claim: Where a state rent control act covers the premises, Section 106 TPA termination may not suffice and statutory grounds/procedures govern
claims.note: Coverage depends on state, premises type, and rent level; flagged as [VERIFY: applicable state rent control act] in statutory_requirements and never asserted in any regex or scenario.

claims.claim: State rent legislation (e.g., Maharashtra Rent Control Act, 1999) penalises a landlord's withdrawal of essential supplies such as water and electricity
claims.note: Believed correct for Maharashtra (commonly cited as Section 29) but the section is unverified; written only as a [VERIFY] tag and the specific section is blocked from drafts by forbidden pattern no_unverified_rent_act_sections.

claims.claim: Security-deposit refund demands rest on contract; no statutory citation is required
claims.note: Drafts cite the tenancy or leave-and-licence agreement clause and the vacation/handover date instead of a statute.

claims.claim: A landlord cannot evict by self-help (lockout, utility disconnection, harassment); dispossession requires due legal process
claims.note: Settled position used only to frame the from_tenant scenarios (illegal_lockout_hyderabad, water_electricity_cut_pune, harassment_for_vacation_delhi); no specific section is cited anywhere in the package.

claims.claim: The Model Tenancy Act, 2021 is a model law and not directly operative central legislation, so drafts must not cite it as binding
claims.note: Encoded as forbidden pattern no_model_tenancy_act. States that enacted versions have their own state acts, which drafts may name only if separately verified.

claims.claim: Section numbers of state rent control acts used in drafts are unverified and must not appear in training data
claims.note: Precautionary design choice encoded as forbidden pattern no_unverified_rent_act_sections; naming an act generally (without a section) remains permitted.

claims.claim: A compliance window of 15 or 30 days is the customary demand period in Indian landlord-tenant notices; 15 days aligns with Section 106 TPA for termination
claims.note: Encoded as the notice_period_days choice field and the compliance_window regex, which requires only a number of days and cites no statute.

consumer_complaint_cpa2019.display_name: Consumer Complaint (Section 35, Consumer Protection Act, 2019) before the District Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission

claims.claim: Consumer complaints before the District Commission are filed under Section 35, Consumer Protection Act, 2019
'Consumer' is defined in Section 2(7), CPA 2019
'Deficiency' is defined in Section 2(11), CPA 2019
Limitation for filing a consumer complaint is two years from the cause of action under Section 69, CPA 2019
The adjudicating forum at district level under CPA 2019 is named 'District Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission' (District Commission)
claims.note: Basis of the forum_heading required pattern and the no_old_forum_name forbidden pattern

claims.claim: 'Deficiency in service' and 'unfair trade practice' are statutory terms under CPA 2019
claims.note: Used in the deficiency_or_utp regex as bare terms, with no section numbers baked in

claims.claim: The Consumer Protection Act, 1986 stands repealed by the CPA 2019
claims.note: 2019 Act brought into force in 2020; basis of the no_repealed_act_1986 forbidden pattern

claims.claim: 'Defect' is defined in Section 2(10) and 'unfair trade practice' in Section 2(47), CPA 2019
claims.note: Sub-clause numbering not independently confirmed; flagged only inside statutory_requirements, never in a regex

claims.claim: Complainant may institute the complaint where they reside or personally work for gain — Section 34(2)(d), CPA 2019
claims.note: The complainant-residence venue is a known CPA 2019 innovation but the exact clause letter is flagged for verification

claims.claim: District Commission pecuniary jurisdiction limits are set and revised by Central Government notification (ceiling reduced to ₹50 lakh w.e.f. Dec 2021)
claims.note: Notification-dependent; no figure is asserted in any document or regex — encoded only as amount ranges capped at ₹45 lakh

claims.claim: Section 39, CPA 2019 enumerates the reliefs the District Commission may grant (refund, replacement, compensation, costs)
claims.note: Marked [VERIFY] in statutory_requirements per instruction; never used in a regex

claims.claim: A consumer complaint carries a verification clause and is supported by the complainant's affidavit
claims.note: Standard drafting practice before consumer commissions; no statute is cited in the documents for this

claims.claim: Filing fee is payable per the Consumer Protection (Consumer Disputes Redressal Commissions) Rules, 2020
claims.note: Rule name and fee slabs flagged for verification; mentioned only in statutory_requirements

affidavit_general.display_name: General Affidavit (Sworn/Affirmed Before Notary Public or Oath Commissioner)

claims.claim: Statutory citations are not customary in the body of a general notarial affidavit
claims.note: Drafting practice; enforced via the no_statutory_section_citation forbidden pattern

claims.claim: General affidavits are executed on non-judicial stamp paper whose value varies by state and purpose under the applicable State Stamp Act
claims.note: General practice claim only; no schedule entry or article number cited anywhere in the package

claims.claim: Specific stamp duty value for affidavits in any particular state (e.g., Maharashtra)
claims.note: Deliberately not stated; documents carry the '[VALUE] AS APPLICABLE' placeholder instead

claims.claim: Notaries Public are empowered to administer oaths and take affidavits under the Notaries Act, 1952
claims.note: Act named only in statutory_requirements; no section number cited and nothing encoded in regexes

claims.claim: Oaths and affirmations are governed by the Oaths Act, 1969
claims.note: Mentioned in statutory_requirements only; expressly excluded from the document body

claims.claim: Notarial attestation/register formalities (seal, register entry, notarial stamp) per the Notaries Rules, 1956
claims.note: Flagged [VERIFY] in statutory_requirements; the regex gate only requires 'before me' attestation and the officer's designation

claims.claim: A false affidavit may attract prosecution for giving false evidence under provisions of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 replacing IPC ss. 191/193
claims.note: Exact BNS section numbers uncertain; flagged [VERIFY] in statutory_requirements and never encoded in a regex

claims.claim: The Indian Penal Code, 1860 stands replaced by the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (in force 1 July 2024), so IPC citations are blocked by a forbidden pattern
claims.note: Forbidden pattern only blocks IPC mentions; it cites nothing affirmatively

claims.claim: Verification clause recording place, date and that contents are true and correct to the deponent's knowledge and belief is standard affidavit practice
claims.note: Structural convention; no CPC provision cited

claims.claim: 'Do hereby solemnly affirm and declare' is the standard secular affirmation formula for Indian affidavits
claims.note: Drafting convention enforced by the solemn_affirmation required pattern

claims.claim: EWS certificate applications involve a family income criterion
claims.note: Existence of the criterion only; the threshold value is not stated anywhere in the package and the scenario amount range stays conservatively low

claims.claim: Affidavits are sworn before a Notary Public or Oath Commissioner
claims.note: Practice claim underlying the notary_or_oath_commissioner required pattern

employment_offer_termination.display_name: Employment Offer Letter / Termination Letter (Indian Private Sector)

claims.claim: Employees' Provident Funds and Miscellaneous Provisions Act, 1952 is the correct name of the central PF statute referenced in offer letters
claims.note: Act name only; no section numbers or thresholds cited anywhere in the package.

claims.claim: Payment of Gratuity Act, 1972 is the correct name of the gratuity statute referenced in offer letters
claims.note: Act name only; the five-year continuous-service eligibility threshold is deliberately not asserted.

claims.claim: PF and gratuity entitlements depend on establishment coverage and wage/headcount thresholds
claims.note: Package qualifies both benefits with 'as applicable' instead of asserting coverage for any given employer.

claims.claim: State Shops and Establishments Acts may govern notice and termination conditions; the applicable Act is state-specific
claims.note: Documents must carry [VERIFY: applicable state Shops and Establishments Act]; no state statute is named or baked into any regex.

claims.claim: Termination for misconduct ordinarily requires a prior domestic inquiry observing principles of natural justice
claims.note: Drafter instructed to insert a [VERIFY] flag, not assert compliance; prerequisites vary with standing orders and establishment type.

claims.claim: Industrial Disputes Act, 1947 is the correct name of the central statute governing retrenchment of workmen
claims.note: Name appears only inside the [VERIFY] flag text mandated by statutory_requirements.

claims.claim: Section 25F, Industrial Disputes Act, 1947 retrenchment conditions apply to a given termination
claims.note: Depends on workman status, continuous service and establishment; encoded only as a [VERIFY] flag, never as a required_pattern regex.

claims.claim: At-will employment is not a recognised doctrine in Indian employment law, so the phrase is forbidden in drafts
claims.note: Enforced via forbidden_pattern no_at_will to keep US boilerplate out of training data.

claims.claim: 30/60/90-day notice periods and 3/6-month probation are contractual conventions, not statutory mandates
claims.note: Encoded only as field choices; no statute is cited for these values.

mou_two_parties.display_name: Memorandum of Understanding between Two Parties (Indian Contract Act, 1872)

claims.claim: Enforceability of the binding clauses of an MOU rests on the Indian Contract Act, 1872
claims.note: Act named without any section number, per assignment instruction; not encoded in any regex

claims.claim: Arbitration clauses in Indian MOUs customarily invoke the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996
claims.note: Act in force as amended; named without section numbers; dispute regex accepts courts/negotiation as alternatives so no document is forced to cite it

claims.claim: An MOU recording only intent is generally not a binding contract, but clauses expressly made binding (confidentiality, dispute resolution, governing law) can bind the parties
claims.note: Standard drafting practice; ultimate enforceability turns on intention to create legal relations under Indian contract law, so documents use a clarification clause rather than asserting statutory effect

claims.claim: Stamp duty on an MOU varies by the state of execution under the applicable State Stamp Act schedule
claims.note: Schedule entries and duty amounts differ by state (e.g., Maharashtra Stamp Act vs Indian Stamp Act states); flagged [VERIFY] in statutory_requirements and never required or quoted in documents

claims.claim: An MOU is generally not compulsorily registrable unless it creates rights in immovable property
claims.note: Turns on Registration Act, 1908 compulsorily-registrable categories (S.17); flagged [VERIFY] and not cited in generated documents

claims.claim: Title, 'First Party'/'Second Party' designations, WHEREAS recitals, IN WITNESS WHEREOF testimonium, and witness attestation are conventional elements of an Indian MOU
claims.note: Drafting conventions, not statutory mandates; witnesses are customary for MOUs, not legally required, so witness fields are optional

claims.claim: The Indian Partnership Act, 1932 governs general (non-LLP) partnership firms in India
claims.note: Cited in display_name, structural_summary and the act_citation required pattern

claims.claim: The Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996 is the current statute governing arbitration agreements in India
claims.note: Named only in statutory_requirements; the arbitration_clause regex does not require the Act name

claims.claim: Registration of a firm with the Registrar of Firms is optional and effected under Sections 58 and 59, Indian Partnership Act 1932
claims.note: Section numbers customary but flagged per policy; not baked into any regex

claims.claim: An unregistered firm and its partners suffer disabilities in enforcing contractual rights by suit under Section 69, Indian Partnership Act 1932
claims.note: Flagged per assignment instruction; documents should mention advisability of registration without asserting the section as settled

claims.claim: Section 40(b), Income-tax Act 1961 caps deductible interest on partner capital at 12% p.a. simple and limits partner remuneration by book-profit slabs
claims.note: Slabs revised by Finance (No. 2) Act 2024 w.e.f. AY 2025-26; encoded only as the interest field's 6-12% generation range, never cited in a required pattern

claims.claim: Firm names implying government sanction or patronage are restricted (Section 58(3), Indian Partnership Act 1932)
claims.note: Restriction strictly applies on registration; mentioned only as drafting advice with a VERIFY marker

claims.claim: Sections 11-13, Indian Partnership Act 1932 supply default mutual rights and duties of partners absent contrary agreement
claims.note: Mentioned only in statutory_requirements with a VERIFY marker

claims.claim: Stamp duty on a partnership instrument is set by the State stamp law of the place of execution (Maharashtra Stamp Act, Schedule I, in Maharashtra)
claims.note: No article number or rupee rate cited anywhere; left as a VERIFY drafting note

claims.claim: The testimonium ('IN WITNESS WHEREOF') and attestation by two witnesses are conventional execution formalities, not statutory mandates
claims.note: witness_testimonium required pattern enforces convention only, no statute cited

reply_to_legal_notice.display_name: Reply to Legal Notice (on behalf of the Noticee)

claims.claim: The WITHOUT PREJUDICE rubric and express reservation of rights in a reply to a legal notice are customary drafting practice, not statutory mandates
claims.note: Encoded as required_patterns containing no statutory citation; statutory_requirements expressly label it customary

claims.claim: Para-wise reply with specific denials of each adverse allegation and preliminary objections is the conventional structure of an Indian reply to a legal notice
claims.note: Drafting convention only; drives para_wise_reply, denial_language and preliminary_objections patterns

claims.claim: Statutory citations are generally unnecessary in a reply to a legal notice
claims.note: Drafting custom; no Act or section is baked into any required_pattern regex

claims.claim: Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881 is the correct and current name of the Act governing cheque dishonour allegations
claims.note: Act name only, used generally for cheque_bounce_allegation replies; no section number appears anywhere in the package

claims.claim: Specific NI Act defence provisions (statutory presumption of legally enforceable debt and its rebuttal) underpin security-cheque and no-enforceable-debt defences
claims.note: Commonly associated with ss.118/139 but section numbers deliberately omitted; flagged [VERIFY] in statutory_requirements

claims.claim: Validity of an eviction/quit notice period may turn on Transfer of Property Act 1882 s.106 or applicable state rent legislation (e.g., Maharashtra Rent Control Act 1999)
claims.note: Appears only behind a [VERIFY] tag in statutory_requirements; eviction scenarios rest on the contractual notice period instead

claims.claim: Consumer Protection Act, 2019 is the statute a consumer grievance notice would typically invoke
claims.note: Almost certainly current (in force since 2020) but marked VERIFY per policy; appears only behind a [VERIFY] tag, never in a regex

claims.claim: A stale money demand may be resisted as time-barred (limitation)
claims.note: General principle used in scenario money_demand_time_barred; no limitation period, Act or section is cited anywhere

claims.claim: Stop-payment for non-delivery of goods and prior discharge of the debt are pleadable defences to a cheque dishonour allegation
claims.note: Fact- and case-law-dependent; used only as scenario fact patterns, not asserted as settled law in requirements or regexes
